elements.sent-2411-001: In context learning (ICL) is an attractive method of solving a wide range of problems.
elements.sent-2411-002: Inspired by \cite{garg:etal:2022 , we look closely at ICL in a variety of train and test settings for several transformer models of different sizes trained from scratch.}
elements.sent-2411-003: Our study complements prior work by pointing out several systematic failures of these models to generalize to data not in the training distribution, thereby showing some limitations of ICL.
elements.sent-2411-004: We find that models adopt a strategy for this task that is very different from standard solutions.
elements.sent-2411-005: In-context learning (ICL) \cite{brown:etal:2020 promises to make interacting with LLMs easy and accessible.}
elements.sent-2411-006: ICL enables the model to learn a task from a prompt with instructions and a few examples at inference time, without any adjustment of the model's parameters from pretraining.
elements.sent-2411-007: While there have been theoretical reconstructions of ICL, there have been few studies on exactly how ICL works in practice.
elements.sent-2411-008: However, ICL depends on a model's pretraining as \cite{garg:etal:2022 have shown; so doing an in depth analysis of this feature of LLMs is difficult.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Hence, most of analysis done on how ICL works are done on small models and simple tasks.
elements.sent-2411-010: Inspired by \cite{garg:etal:2022 , we investigated whether smaller LLMs with transformer architectures ICL the class ${\cal L}$ of linear functions.}
elements.sent-2411-011: While \cite{garg:etal:2022 answer ``yes'', we provide a more nuanced answer based on a deeper analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-012: We have studied the 1 dimensional case with functions for over 30 models, from transformer architectures with 1 attention head (AH) and 1 MLP layer up 12 MLP layers and 8 AH.
elements.sent-2411-013: We also studied small attention-only models \cite{olsson:etal:2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Since we are interested in whether transformer models can ICL and if so how, even small transformer models are relevant, indeed essential since such an investigation requires training from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-016: Several recent papers claim that Transformer based models trained from scratch can ICL linear functions with performances close to algorithms such as Least Squares, and that a transformer can implement this algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-017: We show that the models we tested do not do this; they also failed to generalize and to provide robust predictions beyond their training data.
elements.sent-2411-018: In particular, all our transformer models failed to ICL the concept of a strictly increasing or strictly decreasing linear function, even over larger intervals in $\mathbb{R}$.
elements.sent-2411-019: We replicated this behavior for several training and test distributions.
elements.sent-2411-020: We trained transformers on different distributions various Gaussian, Bimodal and Uniform distributions.
elements.sent-2411-021: Our experiments show that all our models trained from scratch have `boundary values" ($B, -B$) for prompts $x_i$; when $f(x_i) > B$ or $< -B$, model performance degrades substantially.
elements.sent-2411-022: Training on uniform distributions makes this particularly clear.
elements.sent-2411-023: All our transformer models solve task of ICL linear function by learning a projection from ``nearby" sequences of points in the training data; Section 5 provides a mathematical formulation of what we think the models are doing.
elements.sent-2411-024: The exact projection depends upon the training distribution.
elements.sent-2411-025: The models do not implement linear regression or an algorithm like linear interpolation.

elements.sent-2411-051: Following \cite{garg:etal:2022 we trained several small decoder only transformer models from scratch to perform in-context learning of linear functions.\footnote{Our code can be found in https://anonymous.4open.science/r/incontext-learning-556D/ We set the number of layers (L) from 1 to 6, and attention heads (AH) from 1 to 4.}}

elements.subsection-2411-002: Replicating Section 4.1 results for models trained on other distributions

elements.subsection-2411-006: Taking stock: what are the models learning
elements.sent-2411-141: To ICL \ ${\cal L}$, we expected a transformer model given $(x_1,f(x_1),...,x_n), ?)$ to perform a linear regression.
elements.sent-2411-142: The hypothesis and theoretical constructions of \cite{akyurek:etal:2022,vonoswald:etal:2023 shows that transformer models can perform linear regression with 1 step of gradient descent.}
elements.sent-2411-143: If that were the case, the models should generalize without difficulty.
elements.sent-2411-144: But this is clearly not what we observed.
elements.sent-2411-145: Error rates depend on training distributions $D_F, D_I$ and on the distance of the target function's values from the majority of the data points in the model's training.
elements.sent-2411-146: We've also demonstrated model sensitivity to the entire sequence of ICL prompts.
elements.sent-2411-147: This shows that the models did not learn to use linear regression to solve this task.
elements.sent-2411-148: All our models failed to learn the concept of a strictly monotone increasing or decreasing linear function in ${\cal L}$ over arbitrarily large or at least many large intervals of $\mathbb{R}$.\footnote{This makes sense in terms of \cite{asher:etal:2023 's characterization of learnability.}}
elements.sent-2411-149: The concept of a strictly monotone increasing or decreasing linear function describes a $\Pi^0_1$ set in the Borel hierarchy which \cite{asher:etal:2023 show is not learnable using ordinary LLM assumptions.}
elements.sent-2411-150: The lack of generalizability might suggest our models overfit the data.
elements.sent-2411-151: However, the pretraining data has no noise, and it's too large to be memorized by our models (our largest models with 256 size embeddings have $ < 10^7$ parameters; each parameter would have to encode on average sequences for over 100 different functions).

elements.sent-2411-187: In this paper we have shown a systematic failure case of decoder-only transformer models of various sizes (up to 9.5 million parameters) and architectures.
elements.sent-2411-188: All models failed to learn robustly the class of linear functions on non-noisy data, a task which is entirely determined by only two points and involves a trivial mathematical operation that has been documented to be by construction learnable by LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-189: However, the models did learn something different that enabled them to approximate linear functions over intervals where their training gave lots of examples.
elements.sent-2411-190: Rather than learning a standard algorithm for the task, these models instead perform a kind of projection from close sequences seen during the training.
elements.sent-2411-191: Our investigations perforce focus on relatively small models.
elements.sent-2411-192: But our study highlights a broader issue with ICL: the gap between what LLMs can learn and what they actually learn.
elements.sent-2411-193: Much larger models also face this limitation.
elements.sent-2411-194: The minimality of our examples and the capacity to easily train the models from scratch is actually a key strength of our study.
elements.sent-2411-195: We hope this contribution will inspire further research into what transformer-based models are actually doing on ICL tasks.

elements.section-2411-012: Failure to generalize to longer prompt sequences
elements.sent-2411-303: In-context learning (ICL) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for easily adapting Large Language Models (LLMs) to various tasks.
elements.sent-2411-304: However, our understanding of how ICL works remains limited.
elements.sent-2411-305: We explore a simple model of ICL in a controlled setup with synthetic training data to investigate ICL of univariate linear functions.
elements.sent-2411-306: We experiment with a range of GPT-2-like transformer models trained from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-307: Our findings challenge the prevailing narrative that transformers adopt algorithmic approaches like linear regression to learn a linear function in-context.
elements.sent-2411-308: These models fail to generalize beyond their training distribution, highlighting fundamental limitations in their capacity to infer abstract task structures.
elements.sent-2411-309: Our experiments lead us to propose a mathematically precise hypothesis of what the model might be learning.
elements.sent-2411-310: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit a remarkable capability of learning by analogy, known as in-context learning (ICL) \cite{brown:etal:2020 , promising users to adapt a model to a specific task without requiring costly fine-tuning.}
elements.sent-2411-311: This approach is especially useful for NLP researchers and users who seek to experiment with LLMs but lack the computational resources required to fine-tune or train these models from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-312: Despite several theoretical reconstructions of ICL, there have been few studies on exactly how ICL works in practice.
elements.sent-2411-313: In this work, we address this gap by focusing on a simple mathematical task, which is learning in-context univariate linear functions, where the training regime and data are straightforward and clean.
elements.sent-2411-314: The model is given in-context examples at inference time in a next-token-prediction format \cite{brown:etal:2020 ; given a prompt containing a task input-output examples $(x_1,f(x_1),..,x_n, ?)$, the model is asked to generate a value $\fh(x_n)$ approximating $f(x_n)$.}
elements.sent-2411-315: Inspired by \cite{garg:etal:2022 , we investigated whether smaller LLMs with transformer architectures ICL the class ${\cal L}$ of univariate 1 dimensional linear functions.}
elements.sent-2411-316: While \cite{garg:etal:2022 answer ``yes'', we think the answer really depends on what one means by ``learning ${\cal L}$'': if ICL ${\cal L}$ implies learning the function $f(x) = ax +b$ from data points in the prompt, which is clearly possible using a variety of techniques, then we answer "no"; if one means that the model can approximate the plot of a linear function arbitrarily well over a limited interval---what we call {\em ICL ${\cal L}$ plots over definite intervals ---then we answer "yes".}}
elements.sent-2411-317: Making this point clearly helps us understand ICL better.
elements.sent-2411-318: We studied ICL in over 30 models, from transformer architectures with 1 attention head (AH) and 1 MLP layer up to 12 MLP layers and 8 AH, as well as small attention-only models \cite{olsson:etal:2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-319: Since ICL depends on a model's pretraining, such an investigation requires training from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-320: This means we are perforced limited to examining small models.
elements.sent-2411-322: Recent research suggests that Transformer based models ICL linear functions with algorithms like linear regression or Newton's method; we show this not to be the case, varying a variety of distributions to sample functions and values $x_i$ between training and testing.
elements.sent-2411-323: Models fail to generalize or to provide robust predictions beyond their training data---in particular they fail to find the abstract form of a linear function that applies to arbitrary $x_i \in \mathbb{R}$.. 2.
elements.sent-2411-324: All our models, on all training distributions have `boundary values" ($B_{- , B_{+}$) for prompts $x_i$.}
elements.sent-2411-325: Training with uniform distributions make this particularly clear.
elements.sent-2411-326: When $f(x_i) > B_{+}$ or $< B_{-}$, model performance degrades substantially.
elements.sent-2411-327: Boundary values are crucial for understanding ICL and have important implications for ICL in general; users will need to know the training data for $M$ in order to use $M$ to make predictions with confidence.. 3.
elements.sent-2411-328: Our experiments isolate important bounds on prompt size--namely, that prompts in ICL inference must not exceed in size prompts used in training.. 4.

elements.sent-2411-455: We explore in-context learning (ICL), a popular paradigm for inference with Large Language Models (LLMs), in a controlled experimental setup using synthetic training data.
elements.sent-2411-456: Using a range of small transformer models trained from scratch, we focus on a mathematical task with simple yet precise prompts: learning a linear function $f$ from a sequence of inputs $x_i$ and their corresponding function values $f(x_i)$.
elements.sent-2411-457: Our findings challenge the prevailing narrative that transformers adopt algorithmic approaches like linear regression to in-context learn (ICL) a linear function.
elements.sent-2411-458: We observe that all models have ``boundary values'' that limit generalizability.
elements.sent-2411-459: While we can extend boundary values with training distributions over a wider range, we lose the precision of models trained on distributions with more restricted ranges.
elements.sent-2411-460: Thus, we see a dilemma for ICL at least in some tasks: either models will lack generalizability or precision.
elements.sent-2411-461: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit a remarkable capability to learn by analogy, known as in-context learning (ICL) \cite{brown:etal:2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-462: This approach is especially useful for adapting a model to a task with very few resources at inference time.
elements.sent-2411-463: Are models actually learning to reason or are they simply memorizing training data, as \cite{gsm-symbolic have suggested for other tasks? We address this gap by focusing on a simple mathematical task, the in-context learning of one-dimensional linear functions, where the training regime and data are straightforward and clean.}
elements.sent-2411-464: This allows us to have greater control over the data, enabling a more effective analysis of the model's ICL behavior.
elements.sent-2411-465: We distinguish between two notions of "learning a function".
elements.sent-2411-466: The first, assumed by the literature since \cite{garg:etal:2022 , says that a model has learned a function if, when sampling values and coefficients of linear functions at test time from the same distribution used in training the model yields close to $0$ expected error.}
elements.sent-2411-467: Our models can perform ICL for linear functions in this sense, which we call {\em ICL}$_1$.
elements.sent-2411-468: On the other hand, another notion, call it {\em ICL}$_2$, might say that learning a linear function requires specifying the parameters $a,b$, where $f(x) = ax +b$, from data points in the prompt, something which is clearly possible using a variety of techniques like linear regression.
elements.sent-2411-469: However, we give strong empirical evidence that none of our transformer models use such techniques or are performing ICL$_2$ of linear functions.
elements.sent-2411-470: The reason our models cannot ICL$_2$ is that, across all training distributions, they have ``boundary values" $B^{- , B^{+}$ that constrain their predictions.}
elements.sent-2411-471: When the inputs $x_i$ and the function $f$ parameters are chosen such that $f(x_i) >> B^{+}$ or $<< B^{-}$, model performance degrades substantially.
elements.sent-2411-472: We investigate the conditions under which these boundary values arise.
elements.sent-2411-473: These specific values, frequently near the maximum and minimum observed during training, indicate that the model has likely memorized them.
elements.sent-2411-474: But while boundary values are defined by training, the models' degraded predictions do not come from the training data but are rather a sort of reasoning-based hallucination.
elements.sent-2411-475: We studied ICL in over 30 models, ranging from transformer architectures with a single layer and one attention head (AH) to models with 12 layers and 8 AHs, as well as small attention-only models \cite{olsson:etal:2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-476: To train our models, we use sequences generated by functions $f(x) = ax +b$ where $a, b$ are sampled from a distribution over function parameters and $x$ is drawn from a distribution over inputs.
elements.sent-2411-477: The model learns an algorithm on that training data, and we then evaluate its predictions on a test distribution at inference time.
elements.sent-2411-478: Since ICL depends on a model's pretraining, such an investigation requires training from scratch, and that has constrained our study to relatively small transformer architectures.. \hidden{.}
elements.sent-2411-479: In order to investigate the learnability of a task by a model using a certain procedure like ICL, we first need to set a definition for learnability.
elements.sent-2411-480: Our work follows a general approach to ICL and learning: a model has learned with prediction $\hat{S}$ a set $S \subset V$ {\color{blue V n'est pas défini , if, when sampling $x$ from $V$ at test time using the same distribution as used during training, the expected error for $ x \in V \wedge \neg (x \in \hat{S \leftrightarrow x \in S)$ is close to $0$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-481: We will call this type of ICL, $ICL_1$.
elements.sent-2411-482: For our task, we apply this definition to each sequence $(x_1, f(x_1), ..., x_i)$ with the prediction $y_i = f(x_i)$.
elements.sent-2411-483: There is another criterion for learning a linear function.
elements.sent-2411-484: We might want to say that a model learns a linear function $f$ just in case it has learned the parameters $a,b$ where $f = ax +b$ or the {\em class form of $f$.}
elements.sent-2411-486: There are several algorithms that can isolate these parameters and provably give exactly any target linear function $f$ and hence $f(x_i)$, given data points in the graph of $f$---e.g., linear interpolation or linear regression.
elements.sent-2411-487: If a model can learn one of these algorithms from training data, as the literature has claimed \cite{akyurek:etal:2022,vonoswald:etal:2023,ahn2023transformers,mahankali2023one , then the model can clearly $ICL_2$ linear functions.}
elements.sent-2411-488: Despite its promise and popularity, this phenomenon is still not fully explored.
elements.sent-2411-489: Further research has offered several reconstructions for how ICL might work for the class of linear functions ${\cal L}$ in transformers.
elements.sent-2411-490: Whereas most prior research has concentrated on what transformer models {\em can or {\em could do on this task, we examine how ICL works in practice under different training and testing distributions in order to establish what transformers {\em actually do.}}}
elements.sent-2411-491: This work is closely aligned with our approach, as is the work of \cite{giannou:etal:2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-492: They propose that ICL is enabled by a learned copying and comparison mechanism for ICL.
elements.sent-2411-493: In Section \ref{sec:learn , we show their proposal does not fit our task.}
elements.sent-2411-494: We investigate transformer models that are trained directly on an in-context learning objective.
elements.sent-2411-495: We trained several small decoder-only transformer models from scratch to perform ICL of linear functions.\footnote{Our code can be found in https://github.com/omyokun/re-examining-LF-ICL Our models feature from 1 to 18 layers (L) and from 1 to 8 attention heads (AH) with an embedding size of 64 to 256.}
elements.sent-2411-496: The model's task is to predict the next value for $f(x_i)$ through a prompt of type $(x_1,f(x_1),...,x_i)$.
elements.sent-2411-497: To train the model to perform ICL ${\cal L}$, we looked for a $\theta^{*}$ that optimizes the following auto-regressive objective: \begin{equation}\theta^{*} = \argmin _\theta E_{x_i \in D_I , f \in D_F} {\left[ \sum_{i=0}^k \ell \left(f\left(x_{i+1}\right), {\cal L}_\theta\left((x_1,f(x_1),\dots,f(x_i), x_{i+1})\right)\right)\right]}\end{equation} where ${\cal L _\theta$ is a ``learner'', $\ell$ is squared error and $f : x \rightarrow ax + b$ is a linear function with $a,b$ sampled from some training distribution $D_F$ for functions.}
elements.sent-2411-498: Samples $x_i$ are sampled from a training distribution for points $D_I$.
elements.sent-2411-499: We note that $f \in D_F$ and $x \in D_I$.
elements.sent-2411-500: At each training step, we choose at random, a function $f \in D_F$ and then a sequence of points $x_i \in D_I$ from a distribution $D_I$.
elements.sent-2411-501: For $D_F$ and $D_I$ we used several distributions: the normal distribution $\mathcal{N (0,1)$, uniform (``rectangle") distributions over specified intervals, and bimodal distributions.}
elements.sent-2411-502: We evaluate the models across various test distributions for both function classes $D^t_F$ and data points $D^t_I$.
elements.sent-2411-504: For testing, however, we sometimes consider scenarios where $D^t_F \neq D^t_I$.
elements.sent-2411-505: To evaluate ICL performance of a model on $(D_I^t, D_F^t)$, we generate a set of $N=100$ functions in $D_F^t$.
elements.sent-2411-506: Our data samples for testing are composed of $N_b = 64$ batches, each containing $N_p = 41$ points in $D_I^t$.
elements.sent-2411-507: For each function $f_j$, in each batch b, for all points, we predict for each $x_k^b$, $k \geq 2$, $f_j(x_k^b)$ given the prompt $(x_1^b, f_j(x_1^b),..., x_{k-1 ^b , f_j(x_{k-1 ^b), x_k^b)$.}}

elements.section-2411-021: Appendix: Results on attention layer only models
elements.sent-2411-651: The 12L8AH attention-only model exhibited strong performance, closely matching that of the 12L8AH model with an MLP.
elements.sent-2411-652: The large two-attention-only layer model with 32 attention heads demonstrated greater robustness compared to the full transformer model (which includes an MLP) with either a single layer and one attention head or two attention heads.
elements.sent-2411-653: A single AL model had only a very limited ICL generalization capability beyond testing on $D^t_F = \mathcal{N (0,1)$ as seen in Table \ref{table:2 .}}

elements.sent-2411-664: Consider the uniform distribution {\cal U (-1,1). \begin{equation}P(x_i - \epsilon \leq X \leq x_i + \epsilon) = \int_{x_i - \epsilon}^{x_i + \epsilon} \frac{1}{1-(-1)} dx = \epsilon\end{equation} However, as $H$ refines the projection $\pi$, $P(\pi(x_i) < f(x_n) + \epsilon) = i^m\times\epsilon^n$, where $i > 0, m,n < 41$.}
